title: Vertical position

infobox.value: or **vertical location** is a [position](/wiki/Position_(mathematics) "Position (mathematics)") along a [vertical direction](/wiki/Vertical_direction "Vertical direction") (the [plumb line](/wiki/Plumb_line "Plumb line") direction) above or below a given [vertical datum](/wiki/Vertical_datum "Vertical datum") (a reference level surface, such as [mean sea level](/wiki/Mean_sea_level "Mean sea level")).
or **vertical separation** is the [distance](/wiki/Distance "Distance") between two vertical positions.
exist for expressing vertical position: depth, height, altitude, elevation, etc.

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Position along a vertical direction above or below a given vertical datum

For the supply chain concept, see [Vertical integration](/wiki/Vertical_integration "Vertical integration").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/fa/Seven_Summits_Infographic_%28en%29.svg/250px-Seven_Summits_Infographic_%28en%29.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Seven_Summits_Infographic_(en).svg)

**Vertical position** or **vertical location** is a [position](/wiki/Position_(mathematics) "Position (mathematics)") along a [vertical direction](/wiki/Vertical_direction "Vertical direction") (the [plumb line](/wiki/Plumb_line "Plumb line") direction) above or below a given [vertical datum](/wiki/Vertical_datum "Vertical datum") (a reference level surface, such as [mean sea level](/wiki/Mean_sea_level "Mean sea level")).
**Vertical distance** or **vertical separation** is the [distance](/wiki/Distance "Distance") between two vertical positions.
Many **vertical coordinates** exist for expressing vertical position: depth, height, altitude, elevation, etc.
Points lying on an [equigeopotential](/wiki/Equigeopotential "Equigeopotential") surface are said to be on the same **vertical level**, as in a [water level](/wiki/Water_level_(device) "Water level (device)").
A [function](/wiki/Function_(mathematics) "Function (mathematics)") with [domain](/wiki/Domain_of_a_function "Domain of a function") along the vertical line is called a *vertical distribution* or *vertical profile*.

## Definitions

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Vertical_position&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Definitions")]

The [International Organization for Standardization](/wiki/International_Organization_for_Standardization "International Organization for Standardization") (ISO), more specifically [ISO 19111](/wiki/Spatial_reference_system "Spatial reference system"), offers the following two definitions:[[1]](#cite_note-ISO6709:2008-1)

* *depth*: "distance of a point from a chosen reference surface measured downward along a line perpendicular to that surface."[[1]](#cite_note-ISO6709:2008-1)
* *height*: "distance of a point from a chosen reference surface measured upward along a line perpendicular to that surface";[[1]](#cite_note-ISO6709:2008-1)

[ISO 6709](/wiki/ISO_6709 "ISO 6709") (2008 version) makes the following additional definition:

* *[altitude](/wiki/Altitude "Altitude")*: "height where the chosen reference surface is mean sea level"[[1]](#cite_note-ISO6709:2008-1)

The [International Civil Aviation Organization](/wiki/International_Civil_Aviation_Organization "International Civil Aviation Organization") (ICAO) offers similar definitions:[[2]](#cite_note-annex4-2)

* *[altitude](/wiki/Altitude "Altitude")*: "the vertical distance of a level, a point or an object considered as a point, measured from the mean sea level (MSL);"[[2]](#cite_note-annex4-2)
* *height*: "the vertical distance of a level, a point or an object considered as a point, measured from a specific datum."[[2]](#cite_note-annex4-2)

ICAO further defines:

* *[elevation](/wiki/Elevation "Elevation")*: "the vertical distance of a point or a level, on or affixed to the surface of the earth, measured from mean sea level."[[2]](#cite_note-annex4-2)

I.e., elevation would be the altitude of an earth-bound feature, such as the ground or a building.

### Derived quantities

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Vertical_position&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Derived quantities")]

Further information: [Heights in geodesy](/wiki/Heights_in_geodesy "Heights in geodesy")

Several [physical quantities](/wiki/Physical_quantity "Physical quantity") may be defined based on the definitions above:

* [Depth below seafloor](/wiki/Depth_below_seafloor "Depth below seafloor")
* [Depth in a well](/wiki/Depth_in_a_well "Depth in a well")
* [Drying height](/wiki/Drying_height "Drying height")
* [Dynamic height](/wiki/Dynamic_height "Dynamic height")
* [Ellipsoidal height](/wiki/Ellipsoidal_height "Ellipsoidal height")
* [Geocentric altitude](/wiki/Geocentric_altitude "Geocentric altitude")
* [Geopotential](/wiki/Geopotential "Geopotential")
* [Height above mean sea level](/wiki/Height_above_mean_sea_level "Height above mean sea level")
* [Height above average terrain](/wiki/Height_above_average_terrain "Height above average terrain")
* [Height above ground level](/wiki/Height_above_ground_level "Height above ground level")
* [Measured depth](/wiki/Measured_depth "Measured depth")
* [Normal height](/wiki/Normal_height "Normal height")
* [Orthometric height](/wiki/Orthometric_height "Orthometric height")
* [Thickness (geology)](/wiki/Thickness_(geology) "Thickness (geology)")
* [True vertical depth](/wiki/True_vertical_depth "True vertical depth")

### Units

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Vertical_position&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Units")]

Vertical distance quantities, such as [orthometric height](/wiki/Orthometric_height "Orthometric height"), may be expressed in various units: [metres](/wiki/Metres "Metres"), [feet](/wiki/Foot_(unit) "Foot (unit)"), etc.

Certain vertical coordinates are not based on [length](/wiki/Length "Length"), for example, [geopotential numbers](/wiki/Geopotential_number "Geopotential number") have units of m2/s2.
Normalization by a constant nominal gravity value (units of m/s2) yields units of metre, as in [geopotential height](/wiki/Geopotential_height "Geopotential height") (based on [standard gravity](/wiki/Standard_gravity "Standard gravity")) or [dynamic height](/wiki/Dynamic_height "Dynamic height") (based on [normal gravity](/wiki/Normal_gravity "Normal gravity") at 45 degrees latitude). Despite the [physical dimension](/wiki/Physical_dimension "Physical dimension") and [unit of length](/wiki/Unit_of_length "Unit of length"), the vertical coordinate does not represent distance in [physical space](/wiki/Physical_space "Physical space"), as would be measured with a ruler or [tape measure](/wiki/Tape_measure "Tape measure").
Sometimes a ***geopotential metre*** (symbol gpm or m') or *dynamic metre* is introduced for emphasis.[[3]](#cite_note-3)[[4]](#cite_note-Bjerknes1910-4) However, this practice is not acceptable with the [International System of Units](/wiki/International_System_of_Units "International System of Units") (SI).[[a]](#cite_note-6)

Another non-SI unit is the *vertical metre*, introduced when there may be confusion between vertical, horizontal, or [slant distances](/wiki/Slant_distance "Slant distance").
It is used for distance climbed during sports such as [mountaineering](/wiki/Mountaineering "Mountaineering"), [skiing](/wiki/Alpine_skiing "Alpine skiing"), [hiking](/wiki/Hiking "Hiking"), [running](/wiki/Running "Running") or [cycling](/wiki/Cycling "Cycling").[[6]](#cite_note-7)
In German-speaking countries the abbreviation 'Hm' for *Höhenmeter* ("height metre") is used; if it is preceded by a '±' it refers to the [cumulative elevation gain](/wiki/Cumulative_elevation_gain "Cumulative elevation gain").

## Determination

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Vertical_position&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Determination")]

Various instruments and techniques may be used for measuring or determining vertical position:

* [Altimeter](/wiki/Altimeter "Altimeter")
* [Bathymetry](/wiki/Bathymetry "Bathymetry")
* [Benchmark (surveying)](/wiki/Benchmark_(surveying) "Benchmark (surveying)")
* [Depth gauge](/wiki/Depth_gauge "Depth gauge")
* [Depth sounding](/wiki/Depth_sounding "Depth sounding")
* [Hypsometer](/wiki/Hypsometer "Hypsometer")
* [Topography](/wiki/Topography "Topography")
* [Tide gauge](/wiki/Tide_gauge "Tide gauge")
* [Water level (device)](/wiki/Water_level_(device) "Water level (device)")

## Phenomena

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Vertical_position&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Phenomena")]

Many physical phenomena are related to vertical position, as driven by [gravity](/wiki/Gravity "Gravity"):

* [Hydraulic head](/wiki/Hydraulic_head "Hydraulic head")
  + [Stage (hydrology)](/wiki/Stage_(hydrology) "Stage (hydrology)")
* [Isostasy](/wiki/Isostasy "Isostasy")
* [Mean sea level](/wiki/Mean_sea_level "Mean sea level")
  + [Geoid](/wiki/Geoid "Geoid")
  + [Sea surface height](/wiki/Sea_surface_height "Sea surface height")
* [Temperature lapse rate](/wiki/Temperature_lapse_rate "Temperature lapse rate")
* [Terrain](/wiki/Terrain "Terrain")
  + [Digital terrain model](/wiki/Digital_terrain_model "Digital terrain model")
  + [Topographic prominence](/wiki/Topographic_prominence "Topographic prominence")
* [Vertical displacement](/wiki/Vertical_displacement "Vertical displacement")
  + [Post-glacial rebound](/wiki/Post-glacial_rebound "Post-glacial rebound")
  + [Subsidence](/wiki/Subsidence "Subsidence")
  + [Tectonic uplift](/wiki/Tectonic_uplift "Tectonic uplift")
* [Vertical pressure variation](/wiki/Vertical_pressure_variation "Vertical pressure variation")

## Notes

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Vertical_position&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Notes")]

1. **[^](#cite_ref-6)** The Guide for the Use of the International System of Units, section 7.5 (Unacceptability of mixing information with units), states:[[5]](#cite_note-Thompson_Taylor_2018-5)
   > When one gives the value of a quantity, any information concerning the quantity or its conditions of measurement must be presented in such a way as not to be associated with the unit.
